subject: English
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – The Inner World of Amanda
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: Don’t bite your nails, Amanda! / Don’t hunch your shoulders, Amanda! / Stop that slouching and sit up straight, Amanda! The speaker is a parental figure who is deeply concerned with Amanda’s posture and habits. The constant use of imperatives and the exclamation marks show a commanding and critical tone.
questions: What two physical habits is Amanda told to stop?
What instruction is given regarding her posture?
How many times is Amanda's name called in this short passage?
What does the exclamation mark suggest about the speaker’s tone?
Identify a word that means 'to sit or stand in a lazy, drooping way'.

passages.paragraph: (There is a languid, emerald sea, / where the sole inhabitant is me— / a mermaid, drifting blissfully.) In her first fantasy, Amanda retreats to the ocean. She imagines herself as a solitary creature of the sea, moving slowly and peacefully without anyone to disturb her silence.
questions: What color is the sea in Amanda's imagination?
Who is the 'sole inhabitant' of this sea?
What mythical creature does Amanda imagine herself to be?
What does the word 'languid' suggest about the water?
How is Amanda moving through the sea?

passages.paragraph: Did you finish your homework, Amanda? / Did you tidy your room, Amanda? / I thought I told you to clean your shoes, Amanda! The instructions shift from Amanda’s body to her daily responsibilities. The parent is checking on chores and schoolwork, emphasizing order and obedience in the household.
questions: What three tasks does the speaker ask about?
What was Amanda specifically told to do regarding her footwear?
What does this paragraph reveal about the parent's expectations?
Identify the word that means 'to make neat or organized'.
How does this stanza contrast with the previous one about the mermaid?

passages.paragraph: (I am an orphan, roaming the street. / I pattern soft dust with my hushed, bare feet. / The silence is golden, the freedom is sweet.) Amanda’s second fantasy is more extreme. She imagines having no parents at all, simply so she can enjoy the absolute silence and the freedom to walk wherever she wants without being watched.
questions: What role does Amanda take in this fantasy?
What is she doing with her 'bare feet'?
What two qualities does she value most in this paragraph?
What does 'hushed' suggest about her movement?
Why is it ironic that she wants to be an 'orphan'?

passages.paragraph: Don’t eat that chocolate, Amanda! / Remember your acne, Amanda! / Will you please look at me when I’m speaking to you, Amanda! The parent is now controlling Amanda’s diet and demanding eye contact. The focus on 'acne' shows a concern for physical appearance, which adds to Amanda's feeling of being scrutinized.
questions: What food item is Amanda forbidden from eating?
Why is she told not to eat it?
What behavior is Amanda exhibiting that makes the speaker ask her to 'look at me'?
What physical skin condition is mentioned?
What does 'scrutinized' mean in this context?

passages.paragraph: (I am Rapunzel, I have not a care; / life in a tower is tranquil and rare; / I’ll certainly never let down my bright hair!) In her final fantasy, Amanda identifies with a fairy-tale character. However, she plans to change the story by never letting anyone into her tower, ensuring her peace remains undisturbed.
questions: Which fairy-tale character does Amanda imagine herself as?
Where does she imagine she is living?
What adjectives describe life in the tower?
What does she vow 'never' to do with her hair?
Why does she want to keep her hair up?

passages.paragraph: The poem uses parentheses (brackets) to show the difference between what is being said to Amanda and what Amanda is thinking. This structural choice highlights the total lack of communication between the adult and the child; they exist in two completely different worlds simultaneously.
questions: What punctuation mark is used to separate Amanda's thoughts?
What is the structural purpose of these brackets?
Are the parent and Amanda communicating effectively?
What do the two 'worlds' in the poem represent?
How does the formatting help the reader understand Amanda’s mind?

passages.paragraph: Stop that sulking at once, Amanda! / You’re always so moody, Amanda! / Anyone would think that I nagged you, Amanda! In the final stanza, the parent is worried about public perception. The speaker wants Amanda to look happy so that people don't blame the parent for being too hard on her.
questions: What does the speaker ask Amanda to stop doing 'at once'?
What personality trait does the speaker attribute to Amanda?
What is the parent's concern regarding 'anyone' else?
Identify the word that means 'constantly complaining or urging'.
What is ironic about the last line of the poem?

passages.paragraph: The colors in Amanda’s fantasies are symbolic. The 'emerald sea' and 'golden silence' represent a wealth of peace that her real life lacks. By using bright, precious-sounding colors for her imagination, the poet shows that Amanda’s mind is her only place of true value.
questions: What color is used to describe the sea?
What color is associated with 'silence'?
What do these colors represent for Amanda?
Where does Amanda find 'true value' in her life?
What is 'symbolic' according to this passage?

passages.paragraph: Ultimately, the poem 'Amanda' is about the loss of childhood innocence under the weight of constant social and parental conditioning. It portrays a child who is so overwhelmed by rules that she chooses to withdraw entirely into her own mind to survive.
questions: What is the 'ultimate' theme of the poem?
What causes the loss of Amanda's innocence?
What 'survival' method does Amanda choose?
How does Amanda feel about the rules imposed on her?
Summarize the main message of the poem in your own words.
